eyebrow: Turn inbox ambiguity into something both sides can confirm
headline: Paste the vague request; draft a confirmation sentence.
subheadline: Paste the request exactly as received. I'll restate the expected behavior and boundary in a sentence the sender can confirm or correct.
chatStarter: Rustam, this request landed today, and I cannot act without either overcommitting or asking a dozen unfocused questions.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: A confirmation sentence creates immediate progress and exposes misunderstanding before work expands.

eyebrow: Replace persuasive claims with observable checks
headline: Bring the vendor claim; leave with proof criteria.
subheadline: Paste the claim and the conditions you expect. I'll translate it into observable behavior, evidence to request, and a failure case worth testing.
chatStarter: Rustam, the vendor wants an answer this week, but the demo does not prove how their promise survives our busiest day.
whyItWorks: Proof criteria replace sales confidence with a calm, reusable basis for choosing.

eyebrow: Keep useful output, verify costly claims
headline: Paste the AI output; mark unsupported leaps.
subheadline: Send the output with its source material. I'll separate supported facts, plausible inference, and claims needing verification, then mark the highest-consequence gap.
chatStarter: Rustam, this AI answer sounds coherent, but I may act on a claim I have not actually verified.
whyItWorks: The first exchange preserves useful work while making the riskiest unsupported leap visible.

eyebrow: Recover planning value from the miss
headline: Trace the missed deadline; write a planning correction.
subheadline: Bring the original plan and actual sequence. We'll record the assumption, contradicting evidence, and revised rule that should shape the next estimate.
chatStarter: Rustam, we missed the date yesterday, and blaming execution will not explain why the plan looked believable.
whyItWorks: A written correction recovers planning value from the miss without turning reflection into blame.

eyebrow: Temporary help needs a visible ending
headline: Give the rushed workaround an expiry signal.
subheadline: Describe what the workaround protects and what it costs. I'll define the frequency, failure, or risk that should force review, replacement, or retirement.
chatStarter: Rustam, we shipped this workaround under pressure, and nobody knows when temporary becomes too expensive to keep.
whyItWorks: An expiry signal keeps urgent relief from quietly becoming permanent, ownerless maintenance.

eyebrow: Measure delay before prescribing faster work
headline: Time the slow step; separate wait from work.
subheadline: Send the step and its timestamps if available. I'll split active work, queue time, approval wait, and retry time, then name the dominant delay.
chatStarter: Rustam, this step keeps delaying delivery, but nobody can tell whether processing, waiting, approval, or retries consume the time.
whyItWorks: The timeline identifies the kind of delay, so improvement targets the correct mechanism.

headline: Reframe the feature request around the user's job.
subheadline: Paste the request in the customer's words. We'll identify the triggering situation, current struggle, desired change, and smallest evidence that the job exists.
chatStarter: Rustam, a client wants another dashboard, but I do not know what they cannot accomplish today.
proofLine: Start with a free 15-minute fit check.
whyItWorks: The job reframes feature pressure into a customer outcome that can be tested.

eyebrow: Permission failures often cross invisible owners
headline: Send the permission error; map who owns access.
subheadline: Share the exact error, actor, and resource. I'll map the identity source, granting authority, revocation owner, and the specific access request to send.
chatStarter: Rustam, access fails between systems, and every administrator says another account or team controls the permission.
whyItWorks: An ownership route turns a frustrating error into a precise request someone can answer.

headline: Price the new requirement in displaced work.
subheadline: Paste the requirement and the delivery constraint. Together we'll identify the existing commitment, dependency, or quality check it displaces and make that cost visible.
chatStarter: Rustam, this new request sounds small, but the team has no empty capacity and something else must move.
whyItWorks: Visible displacement makes scope negotiation concrete without automatically rejecting the new requirement.

headline: Write the exclusion line disputed scope needs.
subheadline: Paste the disputed item and promised outcome. I'll draft a sentence stating what is excluded, why, and which evidence would justify reopening it.
chatStarter: Rustam, the client assumes this behavior is included, while our team believes it sits outside the agreed scope.
whyItWorks: A sendable boundary reduces ambiguity while leaving a fair, evidence-based route back into scope.
